content.full_text:
x STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramentot CA 95811 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
'
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
. .. 
Kara K. Ueda 
City Attorney 
City of Los Altos 
500 Capitol Mall, Suite 1700 
Sacramento, CA 95814 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-17-210 
Dear Ms. Ueda: 
March 30t 2018 
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of 
the Political Refonn Act (the .. Act").1 Please note that we are only providing advice under the 
conflict of interest provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions 
such as common law conflict of interest or Section 1090. 
Alsot note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 
FPPC Ops. 71)t and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is 
not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should changet you should contact us for 
additional advice. 
QUESTION 
(I) Is Mayor Prochnow disqualified from taking part in decisions related to the Downtown 
Vision Process? 
(2) Is the Mayor disqualified from taking part in decisions related to the Los Altos Community 
Investments Project? 
(3) If the Mayor is disqualified from participating in the Downtown Vision Process, may the 
City segment decisions related to the process to facilitate the Mayor's participation? 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
_references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


CONCLUSION 
File No. A-17-210 
Page No. 2 
(1) Yes. The Mayor is disqualified because the Downtown Vision Process will have a 
reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on her interest in a commercial rental 
property. 
(2) Yes. The Mayor is disqualified because the Los Altos Community Investments Project will 
have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on her interest in a commercial rental 
property. 
(3) Yes. The City may segment decisions related to the Downtown Vision Process to facilitate 
the Mayor's participation so long as it is done in strict adherence to the Act and FPPC 
Regulations. To ensure compliance, we strongly suggest submitting any proposed 
segmentations to the Commission for review prior to executing them. 
FACTS AS PROVIDED BY REQUESTOR 
Your law firm serves as the City Attorney for the City of Los Altos (the "City''). You are 
requesting advice on behalf of Los Altos Mayor Mary Prochnow regarding whether she may 
participate in decisions relating to either the Downtown Vision Process or the Los Altos 
Community Investments Project. 
Approximately one-third of the businesses operating in the City are located in its downtown 
area commonly referred to as the "Downtown Triangle." This area spans one-tenth of a mile. 
Downtown Vision Process 
The City is currently conducting a Downtown Vision Process (the "Vision Process"). Its 
purpose is to solicit community input on future development in the Downtown Triangle, and based 
on that information, prepare several "development scenario alternatives" to be reviewed and 
approved by municipal decision makers, including the City Council. The chosen "development 
scenario alternative" will serve as the basis for a "Downtown Vision Document" (the "Document"). 
You indicate that the Document will "help guide the type and extent of development in the 
Downtown to achieve the desired vibrancy of the Downtown with the goal of creating a safe and 
welcoming Downtown for the entire community." However, the City does not anticipate that the 
Document will serve as a substitute for a specific plan for the Downtown Triangle. Instead it will 
serve as a high-level planning document that will provide policy objectives and priorities, and "set 
the stage for more refined land use documents and approvals, including the potential adoption of a 
specific plan and/or future zoning code amendments." 
Los Altos Community Investments Project 
The City Council and Los Altos Community Investments, a local real estate developer, 
entered into an exclusive agreement to negotiate terms to convert a public parking lot in the 
Downtown Triangle into a half-acre public plaza/park and office building. The project is 
collectively referred to as the Los Altos Community Investments Project (the "LACI Project"). The 
plaza/park will include open space, a stage and an outdoor meeting space, which the City plans to 


File No. A-17-210 
Page No. 3 
offer for public events and general recreation. It's unclear what types of tenants would occupy the 
office building. 
You indicate that the City plans to add additional parking spaces to a nearby underground 
garage to compensate for those lost in the conversion. While the City has not determined the 
number of spaces it plans to add, it will likely be somewhere between 35 to 79 more than what is 
currently offered in the existing lot. 
The proposed decisions to be considered by the City Council regarding the LACI Project 
include various entitlements necessary for it to proceed, including a potential development 
agreement and other related land use actions. 
Prochnow Relators, Inc. & Main Street Building 
The Mayor has an 80-percent ownership interest in Prochnow Realtors, Inc. and a 50-
percent ownership interest in a commercial building and property located at 164 Main Street, in the 
Downtown Triangle (the "Main Street Building"). Prochnow Realtors, Inc. operates out of the Main 
Street Building along with 12 other commercial tenants. You indicate that most of the tenants, 
including Prochnow Realtors, Inc., do not depend or rely upon foot traffic for their business. 
The Main Street Building is located 625 feet from the nearest point of the LACI Project site. 
ANALYSIS 
Section 87100 prohibits a public official from making, participating in making, or otherwise 
using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a 
financial interest. Interests from which a conflict of interest may arise include the following: 
• Any business entity in which the official has a direct or indirect investment of $2,000 or 
more. 
• Any real property in which the official has a direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or more. 
• Any source of income aggregating to $500 or more provided, promised or received in the 12 
months prior to the decision.2 
• An interest in the official's personal finances, including those of the official's immediate 
family. This is known as the "personal financial effects" rule. 
(Section 87103.) 
You have identified Mayor Prochnow's interests in Prochnow Realtors, Inc. as a business 
entity and the Main Street Building as a business entity and real property. Mayor Prochnow also has 
2 The Act's definition of"income" includes a pro-rata share of any income of any business entity or trust in 
which the individual or spouse owns, directly, indirectly, or beneficially, a IO-percent interest or greater. (Section 
82030(a).) 


File No. A-17-210 
Page No. 4 
interests as sources of income in customers of Prochnow Realtors, Inc. and tenants of the Main 
Street Building who have provided her with $500 or more in the aggregate in the twelve months 
prior to talcing part in a governmental decision. We now analyze whether the Vision Process and/or 
the LACI Project will have a reasonably foreseeable and material financial effect on those interests. 
Foreseeability and Materiality 
A conflict of interest may arise only when the reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a 
governmental decision on a public official's interest is material. (Section 87100.) The standard for 
foreseeability differs depending on whether an interest is explicitly involved in the decision. 
(Regulation 18701.) 
Downtown Vision Process 
If a decision involves the adoption of or amendment to a general or specific plan 
encompassing an official's real property interest, the interest is considered explicitly involved in the 
decision, and the effect of the decision on the interest is deemed both foreseeable and material. 
(Regulation 18701(a); 18702.2(a)(l ).) 
As we advised in the Houston Advice Letter, No. 1-16-095, a decision involving a 
''visioning" process that may lead to the adoption of or amendment of a general or specific plan is 
considered to have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on all real property interests 
within the boundaries of the proposed plan area. While you have specified that the Vision Process is 
not meant to serve as a specific plan, you have indicated that it may lead to the adoption of a 
specific plan for the downtown area. Therefore, consistent with our prior advice in Houston, we 
find that the Vision Process would have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on the 
Mayor's interest in the Main Street Building, and as a result, the Mayor is disqualified from taking 
part in decisions related to the Vision Process. 
LA CJ Project 
Unlike the Vision Process, the decisions related to the LACI Project do not explicitly 
involve the Mayor's interests. Therefore, the effect of those decisions on the Mayor's interests 
would only be deemed reasonably foreseeable if they could be recognized as a realistic possibility 
and more than hypothetical or theoretical. (Regulation 18701(b).) 
Regulation 18702 .1 (b) provides, in relevant part, that a reasonably foreseeable financial 
effect on an official's business interest, including a source of income, is material if: 
"( A J prudent person with sufficient information would find it is reasonably 
foreseeable that the decision's financial effect would contribute to a change in the 
price of the business entity's publicly traded stock, or the value of a privately-held 
business." 
Regulation 18702.2(a) provides, in relevant part, that a reasonably foreseeable financial 
effect on an official's real property interest is material ifit would: 


File No. A-17-210 
Page No. 5 
"(10) Change the character of the parcel ofreal property by substantially altering 
traffic levels or intensity of use, including parking, of property surrounding the 
official's real property parcel, the view, privacy, noise levels, or air quality, 
including odors, or any other factors that would affect the market value of the real 
property parcel in which the official has a financial interest; 
"(12) Cause a reasonably prudent person, using due care and consideration under the 
circumstances, to believe that the governmental decision was of such a nature that its 
reasonably foreseeable effect would influence the market value of the official's property." 
(Regulation l 8702.2(a)(l0) and (12).) 
Finally, in relevant part, Regulation 18702.3, provides that the financial effect on the source 
of income is material if the source is an individual and "the official knows or has reason to know 
that the individual has an interest in a business entity or real property that will be financially 
affected under [Regulation 18702.1 or 18702.1 ]" or if"[t]he source is a business entity that will be 
financially affected under [Regulation 18702.1]." 
The Main Street Building lies within 625 feet of the LACI Project in the Downtown 
Triangle. Despite spanning only one-tenth of a square mile, the Downtown Triangle already 
contains one-third of the businesses located in Los Altos. As proposed, the LACI Project will 
convert an existing public parking lot into a park/plaza and office building. The City plans to use 
the park/plaza for public events and general recreation. 
Given the Downtown Triangle's compactness, the proposed changes to type and intensity of 
use at the project site will likely change the character of the immediate and surrounding areas, 
especially in regards to traffic, parking, and noise. We also find that the impact of adding another 
office building to the Downtown Triangle - a potential competitor for the Main Street Building -
combined with the potential effect on the pedestrian traffic along Main Street could affect the value 
of the Prochnow's rental business. Lastly, while you have indicated that "most" of the current 
tenants in the Main Street Building do not rely on foot traffic, the LACI Project could affect the 
value of any business that does. Thus, we find that the LACI Project would have a reasonably 
foreseeable material financial effect on the Mayor's interest in the Main Street Building. 
Public Generally Exception 
Commonly referred to as the "public generally" exception, Regulation l 8703(a) permits a 
public official to take part in a governmental decision that affects one or more of their interests if 
the decision's financial effect on the interest is indistinguishable from its effect on the public 
generally. (See Section 87103.) In general, an effect on an official's interest is distinguishable from 
its effect on the public generally if a significant segment of the public is affected and the effect on 
the official's interest is not unique when compared to the effect on the significant segment of the 
public. (Regulation 18703(a).) 
For purposes of the exception, 25-percent or more of all businesses or all commercial real 
property within an official's jurisdiction constitutes a "significant segment of the public." 


File No. A-17-210 
Page No. 6 
(Regulation 18703(b).) You have indicated that approximately 33-percent of the City's businesses 
are located in the Downtown Triangle. Given the size and density of the area, it appears that both 
the Vision Process and the LACI Project, will potentially effect a "significant segment of the 
public." 
However, an official may not take part under the public generally exception if there is a 
unique effect on the official. A ''unique effect," for purposes of the exception, includes a 
disproportionate effect on "an official's interests in ... real properties resulting from the cumulative 
effect of the official's multiple interests in similar entities or properties that is substantially greater 
than the effect on a single interest." (Regulation l 8703(c)(3).) In this case, Mayor Prochnow owns a 
rental property with 13 commercial tenants. Based upon the number of rental commercial units she 
operates, we find that the effect of the decisions would be ''unique" as to the Main Street Building. 
Therefore, the public generally exception does not apply in either instance. 
Segmentation 
Lastly, you ask whether the City could segment decisions related to the Vision Process in 
such a way that would permit the Mayor to participate in some, but not all, aspects of the Vision 
Process. 
FPPC Regulation 18706 permits an agency to segment a decision in which a public official 
has a financial interest, to allow participation by the official, provided the following conditions are 
satisfied: 
"( l) The decision in which the official has a financial interest can be broken down into 
separate decisions that are not "inextricably interrelated" to the decision in which the official 
has a disqualifying financial interest; 
"(2) The decision in which the official has a financial interest is segmented from the 
other decisions; 
"(3) The decision in which the official has a financial interest is considered first and 
a final decision is reached by the agency without the disqualified official's 
participation in any way; and 
"(4) Once the decision in which the official has a financial interest has been made, 
the disqualified public official's participation does not result in a reopening of, or 
otherwise financially affect, the decision from which the official was disqualified."3 
You have not provided us with a specific proposal for segmentation or sufficient facts to 
determine if the exception applies. Therefore, we can only advise you generally that the City is 
permitted to segment decisions to facilitate participation by an official who would otherwise be 
disqualified, but that the segmentation must be done in strict adherence to Section 87100 and 
Regulation 18706. Should the City choose to proceed with segmenting a decision related to the 
3 For purposes of this regulation, decisions are "inextricably interrelated" when the result of one decision will 
effectively determine, affirm, nullify, or alter the result of another decision. (Regulation I 8706(b).) 


File No. A-17-210 
Page No. 7 
Vision Process, Mayor Prochnow may wish to seek additional advice before taking part in the 
decision. 
AS:jgl 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
Sincerely, 
Brian G. Lau 
Assistant General Counsel 
/ 
By: 
Adam Silver 
Counsel, Legal Division

sections.analysis:
Section 87100 prohibits a public official from making, participating in making, or otherwise 
using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a 
financial interest. Interests from which a conflict of interest may arise include the following: 
• Any business entity in which the official has a direct or indirect investment of $2,000 or 
more. 
• Any real property in which the official has a direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or more. 
• Any source of income aggregating to $500 or more provided, promised or received in the 12 
months prior to the decision.2 
• An interest in the official's personal finances, including those of the official's immediate 
family. This is known as the "personal financial effects" rule. 
(Section 87103.) 
You have identified Mayor Prochnow's interests in Prochnow Realtors, Inc. as a business 
entity and the Main Street Building as a business entity and real property. Mayor Prochnow also has 
2 The Act's definition of"income" includes a pro-rata share of any income of any business entity or trust in 
which the individual or spouse owns, directly, indirectly, or beneficially, a IO-percent interest or greater. (Section 
82030(a).)  
interests as sources of income in customers of Prochnow Realtors, Inc. and tenants of the Main 
Street Building who have provided her with $500 or more in the aggregate in the twelve months 
prior to talcing part in a governmental decision. We now analyze whether the Vision Process and/or 
the LACI Project will have a reasonably foreseeable and material financial effect on those interests. 
Foreseeability and Materiality 
A conflict of interest may arise only when the reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a 
governmental decision on a public official's interest is material. (Section 87100.) The standard for 
foreseeability differs depending on whether an interest is explicitly involved in the decision. 
(Regulation 18701.) 
Downtown Vision Process 
If a decision involves the adoption of or amendment to a general or specific plan 
encompassing an official's real property interest, the interest is considered explicitly involved in the 
decision, and the effect of the decision on the interest is deemed both foreseeable and material. 
(Regulation 18701(a); 18702.2(a)(l ).) 
As we advised in the Houston Advice Letter, No. 1-16-095, a decision involving a 
''visioning" process that may lead to the adoption of or amendment of a general or specific plan is 
considered to have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on all real property interests 
within the boundaries of the proposed plan area. While you have specified that the Vision Process is 
not meant to serve as a specific plan, you have indicated that it may lead to the adoption of a 
specific plan for the downtown area. Therefore, consistent with our prior advice in Houston, we 
find that the Vision Process would have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on the 
Mayor's interest in the Main Street Building, and as a result, the Mayor is disqualified from taking 
part in decisions related to the Vision Process. 
LA CJ Project 
Unlike the Vision Process, the decisions related to the LACI Project do not explicitly 
involve the Mayor's interests. Therefore, the effect of those decisions on the Mayor's interests 
would only be deemed reasonably foreseeable if they could be recognized as a realistic possibility 
and more than hypothetical or theoretical. (Regulation 18701(b).) 
Regulation 18702 .1 (b) provides, in relevant part, that a reasonably foreseeable financial 
effect on an official's business interest, including a source of income, is material if: 
"( A J prudent person with sufficient information would find it is reasonably 
foreseeable that the decision's financial effect would contribute to a change in the 
price of the business entity's publicly traded stock, or the value of a privately-held 
business." 
Regulation 18702.2(a) provides, in relevant part, that a reasonably foreseeable financial 
effect on an official's real property interest is material ifit would:  
"(10) Change the character of the parcel ofreal property by substantially altering 
traffic levels or intensity of use, including parking, of property surrounding the 
official's real property parcel, the view, privacy, noise levels, or air quality, 
including odors, or any other factors that would affect the market value of the real 
property parcel in which the official has a financial interest; 
"(12) Cause a reasonably prudent person, using due care and consideration under the 
circumstances, to believe that the governmental decision was of such a nature that its 
reasonably foreseeable effect would influence the market value of the official's property." 
(Regulation l 8702.2(a)(l0) and (12).) 
Finally, in relevant part, Regulation 18702.3, provides that the financial effect on the source 
of income is material if the source is an individual and "the official knows or has reason to know 
that the individual has an interest in a business entity or real property that will be financially 
affected under [Regulation 18702.1 or 18702.1 ]" or if"[t]he source is a business entity that will be 
financially affected under [Regulation 18702.1]." 
The Main Street Building lies within 625 feet of the LACI Project in the Downtown 
Triangle. Despite spanning only one-tenth of a square mile, the Downtown Triangle already 
contains one-third of the businesses located in Los Altos. As proposed, the LACI Project will 
convert an existing public parking lot into a park/plaza and office building. The City plans to use 
the park/plaza for public events and general recreation. 
Given the Downtown Triangle's compactness, the proposed changes to type and intensity of 
use at the project site will likely change the character of the immediate and surrounding areas, 
especially in regards to traffic, parking, and noise. We also find that the impact of adding another 
office building to the Downtown Triangle - a potential competitor for the Main Street Building -
combined with the potential effect on the pedestrian traffic along Main Street could affect the value 
of the Prochnow's rental business. Lastly, while you have indicated that "most" of the current 
tenants in the Main Street Building do not rely on foot traffic, the LACI Project could affect the 
value of any business that does. Thus, we find that the LACI Project would have a reasonably 
foreseeable material financial effect on the Mayor's interest in the Main Street Building. 
Public Generally Exception 
Commonly referred to as the "public generally" exception, Regulation l 8703(a) permits a 
public official to take part in a governmental decision that affects one or more of their interests if 
the decision's financial effect on the interest is indistinguishable from its effect on the public 
generally. (See Section 87103.) In general, an effect on an official's interest is distinguishable from 
its effect on the public generally if a significant segment of the public is affected and the effect on 
the official's interest is not unique when compared to the effect on the significant segment of the 
public. (Regulation 18703(a).) 
For purposes of the exception, 25-percent or more of all businesses or all commercial real 
property within an official's jurisdiction constitutes a "significant segment of the public."  
(Regulation 18703(b).) You have indicated that approximately 33-percent of the City's businesses 
are located in the Downtown Triangle. Given the size and density of the area, it appears that both

public." 
However, an official may not take part under the public generally exception if there is a 
unique effect on the official. A ''unique effect," for purposes of the exception, includes a

operates, we find that the effect of the decisions would be ''unique" as to the Main Street Building.

Segmentation 
Lastly, you ask whether the City could segment decisions related to the Vision Process in 
such a way that would permit the Mayor to participate in some, but not all, aspects of the Vision 
Process. 
FPPC Regulation 18706 permits an agency to segment a decision in which a public official 
has a financial interest, to allow participation by the official, provided the following conditions are 
satisfied: 
"( l) The decision in which the official has a financial interest can be broken down into 
separate decisions that are not "inextricably interrelated" to the decision in which the official 
has a disqualifying financial interest; 
"(2) The decision in which the official has a financial interest is segmented from the 
other decisions; 
"(3) The decision in which the official has a financial interest is considered first and 
a final decision is reached by the agency without the disqualified official's 
participation in any way; and 
"(4) Once the decision in which the official has a financial interest has been made, 
the disqualified public official's participation does not result in a reopening of, or 
otherwise financially affect, the decision from which the official was disqualified."3 
You have not provided us with a specific proposal for segmentation or sufficient facts to 
determine if the exception applies. Therefore, we can only advise you generally that the City is 
permitted to segment decisions to facilitate participation by an official who would otherwise be 
disqualified, but that the segmentation must be done in strict adherence to Section 87100 and 
Regulation 18706. Should the City choose to proceed with segmenting a decision related to the 
3 For purposes of this regulation, decisions are "inextricably interrelated" when the result of one decision will 
effectively determine, affirm, nullify, or alter the result of another decision. (Regulation I 8706(b).)  
Vision Process, Mayor Prochnow may wish to seek additional advice before taking part in the 
decision.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: (I) Is Mayor Prochnow disqualified from taking part in decisions related to the Downtown 
Vision Process? 
(2) Is the Mayor disqualified from taking part in decisions related to the Los Altos Community 
Investments Project? 
(3) If the Mayor is disqualified from participating in the Downtown Vision Process, may the 
City segment decisions related to the process to facilitate the Mayor's participation?

CONCLUSION: (1) Yes. The Mayor is disqualified because the Downtown Vision Process will have a 
reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on her interest in a commercial rental 
property. 
(2) Yes. The Mayor is disqualified because the Los Altos Community Investments Project will 
have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on her interest in a commercial rental 
property. 
(3) Yes. The City may segment decisions related to the Downtown Vision Process to facilitate 
the Mayor's participation so long as it is done in strict adherence to the Act and FPPC 
Regulations. To ensure compliance, we strongly suggest submitting any proposed 
segmentations to the Commission for review prior to executing them.

You indicate that the Document will "help guide the type and extent of development in the

The proposed decisions to be considered by the City Council regarding the LACI Project 
include various entitlements necessary for it to proceed, including a potential development 
agreement and other related land use actions. 
Prochnow Relators, Inc. & Main Street Building 
The Mayor has an 80-percent ownership interest in Prochnow Realtors, Inc. and a 50-
percent ownership interest in a commercial building and property located at 164 Main Street, in the 
Downtown Triangle (the "Main Street Building"). Prochnow Realtors, Inc. operates out of the Main 
Street Building along with 12 other commercial tenants. You indicate that most of the tenants, 
including Prochnow Realtors, Inc., do not depend or rely upon foot traffic for their business. 
The Main Street Building is located 625 feet from the nearest point of the LACI Project site.
embedding.first_500_words: x STATE OF CALIFORNIA 1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramentot CA 95811 (916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 ' FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION . .. Kara K. Ueda City Attorney City of Los Altos 500 Capitol Mall, Suite 1700 Sacramento, CA 95814 Re: Your Request for Advice Our File No. A-17-210 Dear Ms. Ueda: March 30t 2018 This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Refonn Act (the .. Act").1 Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest or Section 1090. Alsot note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71)t and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should changet you should contact us for additional advice. QUESTION (I) Is Mayor Prochnow disqualified from taking part in decisions related to the Downtown Vision Process? (2) Is the Mayor disqualified from taking part in decisions related to the Los Altos Community Investments Project? (3) If the Mayor is disqualified from participating in the Downtown Vision Process, may the City segment decisions related to the process to facilitate the Mayor's participation? 1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory _references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. CONCLUSION File No. A-17-210 Page No. 2 (1) Yes. The Mayor is disqualified because the Downtown Vision Process will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on her interest in a commercial rental property. (2) Yes. The Mayor is disqualified because the Los Altos Community Investments Project will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on her interest in a commercial rental property. (3) Yes. The City may segment decisions related to the Downtown Vision Process to facilitate the Mayor's participation so long as it is done in strict adherence to the Act and FPPC Regulations. To ensure compliance, we strongly suggest submitting any proposed segmentations to the Commission for review prior to executing them. FACTS AS PROVIDED BY REQUESTOR Your law firm serves as the City Attorney for the City of Los Altos (the "City''). You are requesting advice on behalf of Los Altos Mayor Mary Prochnow regarding whether she may participate in decisions relating to either the Downtown Vision Process or the Los Altos Community Investments Project. Approximately one-third of the businesses operating in the City are located in its downtown area commonly referred to as the "Downtown Triangle." This area spans one-tenth of a mile. Downtown
